analysis_summary: MOFCOM Announcement 2026 No.18 adds export licensing for high-performance AI inference chips (GPU/NPU), HBM-equipped accelerators, advanced packaging technology transfers, and GDSII tape-out flows for 14nm-and-below logic to overseas foundries. HS codes 8541.40, 8542.31, 8479.50, and 8486.20 are referenced.

proposed_tags.short_description: High-performance AI inference chips now require export licenses from 2026-06-01
proposed_tags.description: From 2026-06-01, export of high-performance AI inference chips—including GPU, NPU, and similar accelerators—requires a dual-use export license when any threshold is met: FP8 compute at or above 4800 TOPS, integrated HBM2e or higher memory, or high-speed interconnects such as NVLink or CXL 2.0 for data-center or supercomputing deployment. Exporters must apply to MOFCOM for a dual-use export license and declare end-user and end-use accurately.
proposed_tags.source_citation: MOFCOM Announcement 2026 No.18; Dual-Use Items Export License Management Framework
proposed_tags.exemptions: Certified consumer terminal devices such as smartphones and tablets are excluded when exported as complete units, but controlled chips exported separately still require licenses.
proposed_tags.risk_scenarios: Cloud-training or inference accelerators marketed for enterprise edge use may still trigger licensing if performance thresholds are met. Shipments lacking accurate end-user/end-use declarations may be held at customs.

proposed_tags.short_description: GDSII and advanced packaging technology transfers may require export controls
proposed_tags.description: The announcement adds controls on transfers of GDSII design files for 14nm-and-below logic chips to overseas foundries, as well as technology materials related to 2.5D/3D IC advanced packaging equipment. Fabless exporters should verify whether tape-out, design file, or packaging know-how shipments require a technology export license even when physical chips are not exported.
proposed_tags.exemptions: Mature-node designs at 28nm and above for general commercial applications may fall outside the controlled scope, subject to end-use review.
proposed_tags.risk_scenarios: Sending updated design databases to offshore foundries for engineering trials can qualify as a controlled technology transfer even before mass production.
